requiredExam.action: Use this maneuver to evaluate insulin resistance or cardiometabolic risk phenotype. Acanthosis supports insulin resistance and prompts metabolic risk counseling.

requiredExam.when_to_perform: When insulin resistance or metabolic syndrome is suspected
requiredExam.diagnostic_target: insulin resistance or cardiometabolic risk phenotype
requiredExam.likelihood_ratio_note: No maneuver-specific LR+/LR- is available in the local validated source metadata; treat this bedside finding as supportive and interpret it with the cited guideline, diagnostic tests, and patient context.
requiredExam.management_change: Acanthosis supports insulin resistance and prompts metabolic risk counseling.
requiredExam.limitations: Inspect neck for acanthosis is supportive for Metabolic Syndrome, not diagnostic by itself; reliability can be reduced by pain, positioning, acute illness, cooperation, body habitus, baseline disability, and examiner technique.

reference_values: Diagnosis commonly requires 3 or more: waist >102 cm men or >88 cm women in ATP III US cut points, TG >=150, HDL <40 men/<50 women, BP >=130/85 or treatment, fasting glucose >=100 or treatment.
Use local laboratory intervals and assay-specific cutoffs; endocrine immunoassays are affected by pregnancy, age, sex, acute illness, binding proteins, biotin, and medication effects.
decision_steps: Apply ATP III-style criteria and count waist, triglycerides, HDL, BP, and fasting glucose abnormalities; three or more supports metabolic syndrome.
Look for secondary contributors and related disease: diabetes, fatty liver, sleep apnea, PCOS, hypothyroidism, medications, and alcohol.
Estimate ASCVD and diabetes risk, then decide whether each risk factor needs lifestyle alone or pharmacologic treatment.
If glycemia reaches diabetes range, route to diabetes diagnosis and complication screening.
Confirm that Metabolic Syndrome is the selected clinical intent, then screen for red flags before routine outpatient testing.
Order the first-line tests in sequence: Waist circumference; Fasting triglycerides, HDL, fasting glucose/A1c; Blood pressure and medication review.

Interpret numeric results against the workup cutoffs and local assay ranges: Diagnosis commonly requires 3 or more: waist >102 cm men or >88 cm women in ATP III US cut points, TG >=150, HDL <40 men/<50 women, BP >=130/85 or treatment, fasting glucose >=100 or treatment.

If the initial result is equivocal, discordant with the phenotype, or affected by medication, acute illness, pregnancy, age, sex, or assay interference, repeat or confirm using the next guideline test before committing to long-term therapy.
treatment_options: Use intensive lifestyle intervention for weight, waist, diet quality, physical activity, sleep, and alcohol/tobacco risk.
Treat hypertension, dyslipidemia, hypertriglyceridemia, and diabetes/prediabetes according to their individual guideline thresholds.
Consider anti-obesity medication or bariatric referral when BMI criteria and comorbidity burden support it.
Coordinate fatty liver, sleep apnea, PCOS, CKD, and ASCVD prevention follow-up.
Treat unstable presentations first using the urgent escalation rule for Metabolic Syndrome; draw time-sensitive labs only if doing so does not delay stabilization.
For confirmed Metabolic Syndrome, choose therapy based on severity, comorbidities, pregnancy/fertility context, contraindications, patient preference, and local specialty pathway.
Monitor treatment response with the condition-specific clinical endpoint plus the most relevant labs or imaging listed in this workup.
Escalate to endocrinology or the relevant specialty when results are severe, discordant, recurrent, treatment-limiting, or require localization, surgery, genetic testing, or high-risk medication.

Metabolic Syndrome: diagnostic frame from guideline-sourced endocrine workup

Key thresholds and interpretation caveats: Diagnosis commonly requires 3 or more: waist >102 cm men or >88 cm women in ATP III US cut points, TG >=150, HDL <40 men/<50 women, BP >=130/85 or treatment, fasting glucose >=100 or treatment. Use local laboratory intervals and assay-specific cutoffs; endocrine immunoassays are affected by pregnancy, age, sex, acute illness, binding proteins, biotin, and medication effects.

Important mimics/exclusions: stress or steroid hyperglycemia, type 1/LADA versus type 2 diabetes, DKA/HHS versus uncomplicated hyperglycemia, hypoglycemia/drug effect, renal disease, infection, pregnancy, and secondary endocrine causes.
3 or more criteria shifts to intensive cardiometabolic risk reduction.
Markedly abnormal glucose, severe hypertension, pregnancy-context concern, or cardiopulmonary symptoms changes disposition from prevention visit to urgent evaluation.
Discordant or borderline endocrine tests should be confirmed and interpreted with timing, assay, medication, pregnancy, and acute illness context before irreversible treatment.

root.description: Activate when structured or extractable context matches Metabolic Syndrome triggers or the clinician selects workup metabolic_syndrome_v1.

endpoint_missing_context.description: Route here when the patient context needed to activate Metabolic Syndrome is absent or cannot be extracted.

parallel_actions: Use intensive lifestyle intervention for weight, waist, diet quality, physical activity, sleep, and alcohol/tobacco risk.
Treat hypertension, dyslipidemia, hypertriglyceridemia, and diabetes/prediabetes according to their individual guideline thresholds.
Consider anti-obesity medication or bariatric referral when BMI criteria and comorbidity burden support it.
Coordinate fatty liver, sleep apnea, PCOS, CKD, and ASCVD prevention follow-up.
Treat unstable presentations first using the urgent escalation rule for Metabolic Syndrome; draw time-sensitive labs only if doing so does not delay stabilization.
3 or more criteria shifts to intensive cardiometabolic risk reduction.
Markedly abnormal glucose, severe hypertension, pregnancy-context concern, or cardiopulmonary symptoms changes disposition from prevention visit to urgent evaluation.

Use local laboratory intervals and assay-specific cutoffs; endocrine immunoassays are affected by pregnancy, age, sex, acute illness, binding proteins, biotin, and medication effects.; Important mimics/exclusions: stress or steroid hyperglycemia, type 1/LADA versus type 2 diabetes, DKA/HHS versus uncomplicated hyperglycemia, hypoglycemia/drug effect, renal disease, infection, pregnancy, and secondary endocrine causes.
